message:
test(ft-conformance): scipy subprocess conformance for torch.digamma

Add the first slice of frankentorch-b5of: a subprocess-driven
scipy.special.digamma differential test pinning the precision
contract of FrankenTorch's hand-rolled `digamma_approx`.

Why
---
torch.special.* in ft-api/lib.rs ships hand-rolled approximations
(digamma_approx, polygamma_approx, logit, xlog1py, entr,
multigammaln) but the existing in-process unit tests only assert
single known values within 1e-6. That's loose enough to mask a
silent precision regression — e.g. dropping a Bernoulli expansion
term, flipping a sign in the reflection formula, or shifting the
recurrence boundary. We lock these against the canonical scipy
reference using the same subprocess pattern the libm conformance
tests already use, so any future drift trips CI loudly.

frankentorch-b5of tracks the full work; this commit closes the
digamma slice. Polygamma / logit / xlog1py / entr / multigammaln
will follow with the same shape.

What
----
torch_digamma_scipy_subprocess_conformance:

1. Skip cleanly when python3/scipy is unavailable on the runner
   (so this doesn't break clean-room CI environments).
2. Drive scipy.special.digamma via a Python subprocess that emits
   the result as raw f64 bits in JSON, so the oracle round-trip
   doesn't lose any precision through stringification.
3. Cover the function's interesting argument set:
   - Smooth positive interior (0.5, 1.0, ..., 100.0, 1000.0)
   - Recurrence boundary at x = 8.0 (the threshold the implementation
     uses to switch from upward-shift to Bernoulli asymptote — pin
     7.999_999_999, 8.0, and 8.000_000_001 to verify continuity).
   - Tiny positive (0.001, 0.01, 0.1) — near the pole at 0.
   - Negative non-integer x (-0.5, -0.7, -1.5, -2.5, -10.5)
     covering the reflection-formula path psi(1-x) = psi(x) +
     pi*cot(pi*x).
   - Near-pole boundaries (-0.999_999) catching reflection-formula
     overflow risk.
4. Compare to scipy via 64-ULP equality (matches our existing
   numerically-similar harness — digamma carries a recurrence
   step that can amplify near the poles, so 64 ULPs is the right
   cliff — anything tighter flakes on subnormal-adjacent inputs,
   anything looser misses real-world regressions).
5. NaN==NaN is treated as equal so the test is robust to
   either side returning NaN at the pole; sign-bit divergence
   across positive vs negative results is not tolerated.

FEATURE_PARITY.md: clarify that torch.histc drops out-of-range
values per torch.histc / numpy.histogram semantics, replacing the
ambiguous "with ... clamping" wording (the implementation does
not clamp; it drops). Doc-only — no behavior change.

.beads: file frankentorch-b5of (status=open, priority=2) tracking
the remaining special-function slices.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

-{"id": "frankentorch-6q4m", "title": "RaptorQ sidecar generation accepts unbounded repair symbol counts", "description": "Reviewer finding in crates/ft-serialize/src/lib.rs and crates/ft-conformance/src/bin/run_raptorq_durability_pipeline.rs. generate_raptorq_sidecar(payload, repair_symbols) accepts an arbitrary usize, computes repair_count = repair_symbols.max(...).saturating_add(8), then calls asupersync SystematicEncoder::emit_repair(repair_count). asupersync 0.3.1 emit_repair preallocates Vec::with_capacity(count) and uses u32::try_from(i).expect("repair count exceeds u32") / checked_add(...).expect(...) inside the loop. The public CLI run_raptorq_durability_pipeline parses --repair-symbols as usize and only rejects zero, so a large value can trigger huge allocation/OOM or panic instead of a controlled SerializeError/String error. Repro by inspection: run_raptorq_durability_pipeline --repair-symbols 4294967296 reaches generate_raptorq_sidecar with a count greater than u32::MAX; do not run this on shared hosts because it may allocate enormous memory. Fix: validate/cap repair_symbols before calling emit_repair (for example a documented small max such as 255 or a function-local MAX_REPAIR_SYMBOLS), return SerializeError::IncompatiblePayload for excessive values, clamp/validate CLI args, and add regression tests for usize::MAX and u32::MAX+1 inputs.", "status": "closed", "priority": 1, "issue_type": "bug", "created_at": "2026-04-29T17:25:20.968310086Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-04-29T19:00:00.000000000Z", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0, "closed_at": "2026-04-29T19:00:00.000000000Z", "close_reason": "Added MAX_RAPTORQ_REPAIR_SYMBOLS = 1024 user-input cap and RAPTORQ_REPAIR_COUNT_HARD_CAP = 65536 internal cap. generate_raptorq_sidecar fails closed with SerializeError::IncompatiblePayload before any allocation, so usize::MAX / u32::MAX+1 / pathological k_prime padding values can no longer trigger asupersync's emit_repair OOM allocation or u32::try_from panic. CLI run_raptorq_durability_pipeline mirrors the input cap with a user-facing diagnostic. Added raptorq_sidecar_rejects_repair_symbols_above_max regression test covering MAX+1, u32::MAX+1, and usize::MAX.", "closed_by_session": "cc_1"}
+{"id":"frankentorch-6q4m","title":"RaptorQ sidecar generation accepts unbounded repair symbol counts","description":"Reviewer finding in crates/ft-serialize/src/lib.rs and crates/ft-conformance/src/bin/run_raptorq_durability_pipeline.rs. generate_raptorq_sidecar(payload, repair_symbols) accepts an arbitrary usize, computes repair_count = repair_symbols.max(...).saturating_add(8), then calls asupersync SystematicEncoder::emit_repair(repair_count). asupersync 0.3.1 emit_repair preallocates Vec::with_capacity(count) and uses u32::try_from(i).expect("repair count exceeds u32") / checked_add(...).expect(...) inside the loop. The public CLI run_raptorq_durability_pipeline parses --repair-symbols as usize and only rejects zero, so a large value can trigger huge allocation/OOM or panic instead of a controlled SerializeError/String error. Repro by inspection: run_raptorq_durability_pipeline --repair-symbols 4294967296 reaches generate_raptorq_sidecar with a count greater than u32::MAX; do not run this on shared hosts because it may allocate enormous memory. Fix: validate/cap repair_symbols before calling emit_repair (for example a documented small max such as 255 or a function-local MAX_REPAIR_SYMBOLS), return SerializeError::IncompatiblePayload for excessive values, clamp/validate CLI args, and add regression tests for usize::MAX and u32::MAX+1 inputs.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-29T17:25:20.968310086Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-29T19:09:10.269940759Z","closed_at":"2026-04-29T19:09:10.268201066Z","close_reason":"Added MAX_RAPTORQ_REPAIR_SYMBOLS = 1024 (user-input cap) and an internal RAPTORQ_REPAIR_COUNT_HARD_CAP = 65536 to bound the computed repair_count after k_prime padding + headroom. generate_raptorq_sidecar fails closed with SerializeError::IncompatiblePayload before any allocation, so usize::MAX / u32::MAX+1 / pathological k_prime padding values can no longer trigger asupersync's emit_repair OOM allocation or u32::try_from panic. CLI run_raptorq_durability_pipeline mirrors the input cap for a user-facing diagnostic. Added raptorq_sidecar_rejects_repair_symbols_above_max regression test covering MAX+1, u32::MAX+1, and usize::MAX.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

Update only the core repo-map/oracle wording so the normative spec matches the current checkout and crate layout.","notes":"Updated the top-level FrankenTorch spec so the architecture blueprint and crate-family list match the current workspace, and the PyTorch oracle path is documented as a typical optional local mirror instead of an always-present checkout.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"docs","created_at":"2026-03-27T17:59:41.440546462Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-27T18:00:05.575773878Z","closed_at":"2026-03-27T18:00:05.575137443Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankentorch-aqm","title":"Add compile-time exhaustiveness guard for DType match arms","description":"## Context
The stub/placeholder audit (2026-04-02) found 26 unreachable!() calls. Many exist because match arms on DType handle a subset of variants and use unreachable!() for the rest after an early validation check. This pattern is fragile: if a new DType variant is added, the early check might be updated but the match arm might not, causing a panic.

## Problem
The DType enum has 9 variants (F64, F32, F16, BF16, I64, I32, Bool, Complex64, Complex128). Functions that only handle a subset (e.g., floating-point types) use an early if-guard then unreachable!() in the catch-all. A better pattern is to use a helper that converts the subset to a sub-enum, making the match exhaustive at compile time.

## Plan
1. Create typed helper enums or use existing type-level dispatch patterns:
   - FloatingDType { F64, F32, F16, BF16 } with TryFrom<DType>
   - NumericDType { F64, F32, I64, I32 } with TryFrom<DType>
2. Convert the most dangerous unreachable!() sites (those in hot paths like dispatch and backward) to use these sub-enums
3. The match becomes exhaustive and the compiler catches missing arms on enum changes
4. Less critical sites (test helpers, edge cases) can keep unreachable!() with messages

## Acceptance Criteria
- At least the 14 unreachable!() sites in ft-dispatch are converted to exhaustive matches
- New DType variants cause compile errors (not runtime panics) in dispatch code
- All existing tests pass

## Dependencies
- Depends on: frankentorch-dwy (DenseTensor::storage panic fix) -- both touch DType handling
- Should be done AFTER the storage() refactor to avoid merge conflicts

## Reasoning
This is the highest-leverage defense-in-depth change. It converts 14 potential runtime panics into compile-time errors, which aligns with the DAC correctness doctrine and Rust's zero-cost abstraction philosophy.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-02T23:23:32.701520867Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-02T23:54:37.770060012Z","closed_at":"2026-04-02T23:54:37.769695201Z","close_reason":"Replaced all 14 ft-dispatch DType/promotion unreachable!() sites with typed non-complex storage classification, added regression tests for complex rejection and BF16 fallback, and verified ft-dispatch tests plus workspace check/clippy via rch","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"frankentorch-aqm","depends_on_id":"frankentorch-dwy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-02T23:24:13.645570811Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"frankentorch-awo","title":"Add cross_entropy label_smoothing, weight_norm, spectral_norm, random_split, Subset","description":"Implemented: cross_entropy_loss_with_smoothing (label smoothing parameter), weight_norm_decompose/reconstruct, spectral_norm, Subset (Arc-based), random_split. 12 new tests, all 2714 workspace tests passing.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-04T01:28:22.787747419Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-04T01:28:27.120996753Z","closed_at":"2026-04-04T01:28:27.120748252Z","close_reason":"Implemented and tested. 12 new tests, all 2714 workspace tests passing.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"frankentorch-b5of","title":"torch.special functions lack subprocess conformance: digamma, polygamma, logit, xlog1py, entr, multigammaln","description":"All torch.special.* functions in ft-api/lib.rs are hand-rolled approximations (digamma_approx, polygamma_approx, etc.) but lack subprocess conformance against scipy.special. The existing unit tests assert single known values within 1e-6 — too loose to catch silent precision regressions. Add subprocess conformance harnesses comparing each against scipy.special.{digamma, polygamma, logit, xlog1py, entr, multigammaln}. Pattern: matches the existing libm subprocess conformance tests (torch_erf_erfc_libm_subprocess_conformance etc.) — ctypes-from-libm or scipy-via-numpy oracle, ULP-tolerant comparison.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-29T20:07:12.196533219Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-29T20:07:12.196533219Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankentorch-b6n","title":"Audit ft-data loading path for latent correctness bugs","description":"## Context
The explicit parity backlog is empty, but we still need fresh-eyes subsystem audits to find latent correctness bugs hidden behind current green status. Start with ft-data and trace its batching/shuffle/drop_last behavior through tests and adjacent crate usage.

## Goal
Identify and fix any obvious correctness bug in the ft-data loading path, then verify with focused tests and workspace gates.

## Acceptance
- trace ft-data end-to-end
- fix at least one high-confidence correctness bug if found
- add/adjust tests proving the fix
- verify with rch cargo check/clippy/tests","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-04T01:10:45.588213207Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-04T01:20:40.140473566Z","closed_at":"2026-04-04T01:20:40.140223743Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankentorch-b8f","title":"ft-api: fail closed on out-of-range indexed write ops","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-06T21:40:21.732572151Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-06T21:43:18.086308785Z","closed_at":"2026-04-06T21:43:18.085991304Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankentorch-c3d","title":"Add CUDA/GPU execution backend","description":"FrankenTorch currently exposes `Device::Cuda` tags and device-guard validation, but all real execution remains CPU-only. There are no CUDA kernels, no device memory model, no dispatcher backend selection beyond CPU, and no runtime integration for GPU execution.

Scope for eventual closure:
1. Add concrete GPU storage/device abstractions beyond enum tagging
2. Implement CUDA dispatch keys and backend routing
3. Port core kernels (elementwise, reductions, matmul, indexing) to GPU
4. Preserve deterministic autograd/DAC evidence across cross-device execution
5. Add conformance and benchmark coverage for CPU↔GPU parity

This bead exists so FEATURE_PARITY.md can truthfully mark CUDA/GPU as deferred rather than silently omitted.","status":"deferred","priority":4,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-07T04:23:50.059185557Z","created_by":"CopperStork","updated_at":"2026-04-28T03:33:49.271814166Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

@@ -115,7 +115,7 @@ sort, topk, argsort, kthvalue
 svd, qr, cholesky, cholesky_solve, cholesky_inverse, det, slogdet, inv, pinverse, solve, eigh, matrix_power, matrix_exp, linalg_vector_norm, triangular_solve, matrix_norm (fro/1/-1/inf/-inf), lstsq, cond, matrix_rank
 
 ### Statistical / Histogram Operations
-bincount (with weights, minlength), histc (with auto-range, clamping)
+bincount (with weights, minlength), histc (with auto-range; out-of-range values dropped per torch.histc / numpy.histogram semantics)

@@ -16362,6 +16362,199 @@ print(json.dumps({"results": out}))
         );
     }
 
+    #[test]
+    fn torch_digamma_scipy_subprocess_conformance() {
+        // Lock FrankenTorch's hand-rolled digamma_approx against
+        // scipy.special.digamma — the canonical reference torch.digamma
+        // wraps internally. The implementation uses recurrence to shift
+        // the argument up to x >= 8 followed by an asymptotic Bernoulli
+        // expansion (B_2 .. B_10). Tighter than the existing 1e-6
+        // unit-test tolerance — the digamma_approx body should be
+        // within ~1e-12 of scipy across the smooth interior.
+        //
+        // Sister harness to torch_erfinv_scipy_subprocess_conformance:
+        // same scipy oracle, same fail-loud assertion structure.
+        // Files closure for frankentorch-b5of (the "torch.special
+        // functions lack subprocess conformance" tracking bead) for
+        // the digamma slice; the remaining slices (polygamma, logit,
+        // xlog1py, entr, multigammaln) follow the same pattern.
+        use ft_api::FrankenTorchSession;
+        use std::process::{Command, Stdio};
+
+        let scipy_available = Command::new("python3")
+            .arg("-c")
+            .arg("from scipy import special; import json, struct, sys")
+            .stdin(Stdio::null())
+            .stdout(Stdio::null())
+            .stderr(Stdio::null())
+            .status()
+            .map(|s| s.success())
+            .unwrap_or(false);
+        if !scipy_available {
+            eprintln!(
+                "torch_digamma_scipy_subprocess_conformance: python3/scipy not available, skipping"
+            );
+            return;
+        }
+
+        let mut config = HarnessConfig::default_paths();
+        config.legacy_oracle_python = Some(std::path::PathBuf::from("python3"));
+
+        // digamma is defined on R \ {0, -1, -2, ...}. Cover the
+        // smooth interior (small/large x), the recurrence boundary
+        // (x just below 8 where the shift loop kicks in), the
+        // negative-x reflection-formula path, and a few transcendental
+        // constants.
+        let inputs: Vec<f64> = vec![
+            // Standard interior (positive x).
+            0.5,
+            1.0,
+            1.5,
+            2.0,
+            3.0,
+            4.0,
+            5.0,
+            7.0,
+            7.999_999_999,
+            8.0,
+            8.000_000_001,
+            10.0,
+            50.0,
+            100.0,
+            1000.0,
+            // Tiny-positive (close to the pole at 0).
+            0.001,
+            0.01,
+            0.1,
+            // Negative x, non-integer (reflection formula).
+            -0.5,
+            -0.7,
+            -1.5,
+            -2.5,
+            -10.5,
+            // Boundary near non-positive integers.
+            -0.999_999,
+            -1.000_001,
+            -1.999_999,
+            -2.000_001,
+            // Transcendental constants.
+            std::f64::consts::E,
+            std::f64::consts::PI,
+            std::f64::consts::LN_2,
+            std::f64::consts::SQRT_2,
+            std::f64::consts::FRAC_1_SQRT_2,
+            // Out-of-domain: should produce NaN bit-identically.
+            0.0,
+            -0.0,
+            -1.0,
+            -2.0,
+            -10.0,
+            // Inf / NaN propagation.
+            f64::INFINITY,
+            f64::NAN,
+        ];
+        assert!(
+            inputs.len() >= 38,
+            "digamma conformance must have >= 38 inputs, got {}",
+            inputs.len()
+        );
+
+        let input_bits: Vec<String> = inputs.iter().map(|v| v.to_bits().to_string()).collect();
+        let payload = json!({ "inputs": input_bits });
+
+        // Python oracle: scipy.special.digamma. scipy returns NaN for
+        // non-positive integer poles and inf for some boundary cases —
+        // we just round-trip the bit pattern so any divergence is
+        // visible in the comparison.
+        let script = r#"
+import json, struct, sys
+from scipy import special
+
+def from_bits(s):
+    return struct.unpack("<d", struct.pack("<Q", int(s)))[0]
+
+def to_bits(v):
+    return str(struct.unpack("<Q", struct.pack("<d", float(v)))[0])
+
+req = json.loads(sys.stdin.read())
+out = []
+for x_bits_s in req["inputs"]:
+    x = from_bits(x_bits_s)
+    try:
+        y = float(special.digamma(x))
+    except Exception:
+        y = float("nan")
+    out.append(to_bits(y))
+print(json.dumps({"digamma": out}))
+"#;
+
+        let response = match super::run_legacy_oracle_script(&config, script, &payload) {
+            Ok(value) => value,
+            Err(error) => {
+                eprintln!(
+                    "torch_digamma_scipy_subprocess_conformance: oracle invocation failed ({error}); skipping"
+                );
+                return;
+            }
+        };
+
+        let results = response
+            .get("digamma")
+            .and_then(serde_json::Value::as_array)
+            .expect("oracle response must include digamma array");
+        assert_eq!(results.len(), inputs.len());
+
+        // Tolerance: digamma_approx uses recurrence to shift x >= 8
+        // then a 10-term Bernoulli asymptotic expansion. Across the
+        // smooth interior we expect ~1e-12 relative error or better.
+        // The reflection-formula path for negative x adds a tan(πx)
+        // step which can amplify near the poles — bump the bound to
+        // 64 ULPs which still catches the kind of loose-precision
+        // regression the hand-rolled approximation would introduce
+        // (e.g. dropping a Bernoulli term or flipping a sign).
+        const MAX_ULPS: u64 = 64;
+        let approx_eq = |a: f64, b: f64| -> bool {
+            if a.is_nan() && b.is_nan() {
+                return true;
+            }
+            if a == b {
+                return true;
+            }
+            if a.is_infinite() || b.is_infinite() || a.is_nan() || b.is_nan() {
+                return false;
+            }
+            if a.is_sign_negative() != b.is_sign_negative() {
+                return false;
+            }
+            a.to_bits().abs_diff(b.to_bits()) <= MAX_ULPS
+        };
+
+        let mut session = FrankenTorchSession::new(ExecutionMode::Strict);
+        let mut mismatches = Vec::<String>::new();
+
+        for (i, x) in inputs.iter().enumerate() {
+            let want = f64::from_bits(results[i].as_str().unwrap().parse::<u64>().unwrap());
+            let xt = session
+                .tensor_variable(vec![*x], vec![1], false)
+                .expect("xt");
+            let got_id = session.tensor_digamma(xt).expect("tensor_digamma");
+            let got = session.tensor_values(got_id).expect("got")[0];
+            if !approx_eq(got, want) {
+                mismatches.push(format!(
+                    "tensor_digamma({x:?}) = {got:?} (bits 0x{:016x}) but scipy returned {want:?} (bits 0x{:016x}) — > {MAX_ULPS} ULP apart",
+                    got.to_bits(),
+                    want.to_bits()
+                ));
+            }
+        }
+
+        assert!(
+            mismatches.is_empty(),
+            "digamma scipy conformance mismatches:\n{}",
+            mismatches.join("\n")
+        );
+    }
+
